Press enter or click to view image in full size

The release of Next.js 16.3 Preview is now available, introducing a landmark set of features centered around Instant Navigations — bringing the snappy, SPA-like feel to server-driven apps, without giving up the benefits of the server-centric model.

• Instant: SPA-like navigations in server-driven apps

• Smarter Prefetching: One shell per route instead of one request per link

• Better DX: Navigation Inspector, Playwright test helpers, and Instant Insights panel

• Deeper AI Integration: New agent skill for Cache Components adoption
Instant Navigations 🚀
The most common complaint about Next.js apps has always been that navigations feel slow. In a traditional server-driven flow, clicking a link means waiting for a full network roundtrip before anything happens on screen.
Next.js 16.3 introduces Instant Navigations — a suite of opt-in tools that fix this. Instead of blocking on the network, you now choose how each route responds to a click:

• Stream with <Suspense> — the user sees a loading shell instantly, with content streaming in after.

• Cache with 'use cache' — the user sees previously cached UI, reused between requests.

• Block with export const instant = false — opt out entirely for routes where you want the traditional server-bound behavior (e.g. a blog post that should never show a loading state).
In the first two cases, navigations are instant — indistinguishable from a SPA click.
Enabling Cache Components
To unlock Instant Navigations, enable the cacheComponents flag in your config:
// next.config.ts
import type { NextConfig } from 'next';const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
cacheComponents: true,
};
export default nextConfig;
This flag reflects Next.js’s ongoing simplification back to its roots: dynamic by default, with no hidden implicit caching. It will become the default in a future major version.
Instant Insights Panel
A new Instant Insights panel in the dev overlay automatically surfaces routes that are blocking navigations. In development, a slow navigation is now treated as an error — so regressions get caught early, not discovered in production.
When a blocking fetch is detected, the panel pinpoints the exact file and line responsible, and offers three actionable fixes right inline — each with a “Copy as prompt” button so you can hand it straight to your agent:

• Wrap the component in <Suspense> so a loading fallback shows instantly

• Add 'use cache' to the component or data fetch

• export const instant = false to opt the route out of instant navigation enforcement
Rethinking Prefetching ⚙️
The old approach
In previous versions, Next.js sent a prefetch request for every link in the viewport as the user scrolled. For a sidebar with 20 chat links, that meant 20 separate network requests — one per link. Many developers noticed this in the Network tab and were rightfully puzzled.
The new approach: Partial Prefetching
Next.js 16.3 borrows a trick from single-page apps. Instead of prefetching per link, it now prefetches a reusable shell per route — once, and cached for the session.

• A sidebar with 20 /chat/[id] links? One prefetch for the /chat/[id] shell.

• Navigation to /dashboard? One prefetch for the dashboard shell.
This is conceptually similar to per-route code splitting in SPAs. Shells are also the foundation for offline navigation — a future release will explore keeping prefetched routes navigable when the network briefly disappears.
Enabling Partial Prefetching
// next.config.ts
const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
cacheComponents: true,
partialPrefetching: true,
};export default nextConfig;
Like cacheComponents, this will become a default in a future major release.
Per-link opt-in
When you need more than the shell prefetched — say, a chat header that should “pop in” instantly — you can opt specific links into deeper prefetching:
<Link prefetch={true} href=”/chat/123">Open Chat</Link>
Next.js won’t try to render the entire route; it renders down to what’s available synchronously or marked with ‘use cache’. This eliminates the old all-or-nothing tradeoff.
By default, per-link prefetching is limited to build-time content. To extend it to request-time cached content:
export const prefetch = ‘allow-runtime’;
Developer Experience ⚒️
Navigation Inspector
A new Navigation Inspector in the Next.js DevTools lets you pause every navigation at the shell to visually inspect what gets prefetched for any route. Once you click “Resume”, the navigation completes and shows the full page. This makes it immediately clear which parts of your route render instantly and which require a network hop.
✍️ Note: Actual prefetching only runs in production mode.
Playwright Test Helper
To prevent regressions in instant routes, Next.js 16.3 ships an instant() test helper for Playwright:
import { expect, test } from '@playwright/test';
import { instant } from '@next/playwright';test('product title is available immediately', async ({ page }) => {
await page.goto('/products/shoes');
await instant(page, async () => {
await page.click('a[href="/products/hats"]');
await expect(page.locator('h1')).toContainText('Baseball Cap');
await expect(page.getByText('Checking inventory...')).toBeVisible();
});
await expect(page.getByText('12 in stock')).toBeVisible();
});
Everything inside instant() runs without waiting for the network — assertions fail if any content requires a roundtrip. The Next.js team is also exploring surfacing these errors at build time, so regressions in instant routes can be caught before deployment.
Agent Skill for Cache Components 🤖
Adopting cacheComponents in an existing app can involve touching many routes. Next.js 16.3 ships a dedicated agent skill that walks you through the migration — identifying blocking routes, suggesting whether to Stream, Cache, or Block, and validating the result with Instant Insights.
To use it, copy the following prompt into Claude Code, Cursor, or your agent of choice:
Install this skill https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/skills/next-cache-components-adoption
and adopt Cache Components in this project using itThe skill installs directly from the Next.js canary repo and gives the agent the full context it needs to make the right call on each route — without guessing.
Real-World Results 🌍
The Next.js team adopted Instant Navigations on v0 ahead of this release. Navigation times dropped significantly after working through the Instant Insights panel route by route.
The chart tells the story clearly. From May 19 through early June:

• p90 dropped from ~0.9s to ~0.35s

• p75 dropped from ~0.5s to near zero

• p50 dropped from ~0.35s to near zero
The team is still optimizing the prefetching improvements, with the expectation that numbers will get even closer to zero across all percentiles.
Recap:

• Instant Navigations (Stream / Cache): cacheComponents: true

• Block a route: export const instant = false

• Partial Prefetching (shell per route): partialPrefetching: true

• Per-link deep prefetch: <Link prefetch={true}>

• Allow runtime cached content: export const prefetch = 'allow-runtime'

• Playwright assertions: instant() from @next/playwright
Getting Started with 16.3
Install the preview today:
npm install next@preview
This is a pre-production release. The Next.js team’s own apps are running it, but use discretion before deploying to real users. Changes are likely before the final stable release.
